definition: Spending more computation at inference, rather than only at training, to improve a model's answers: generating longer reasoning, sampling many candidates, or searching, so accuracy scales with the compute spent per query. Its defining property is a smooth scaling curve, accuracy rises predictably as the budget grows, giving a dial you can set per request according to how much the answer is worth.

body.text: Test-time compute is the deliberate use of extra computation when a model answers a question, as opposed to the computation spent once during training. The traditional picture of a language model is that all the cost is paid up front in training, after which each inference is a cheap single pass; test-time compute breaks that assumption, treating the amount of work done per query as a dial that can be turned up to buy better answers, trading latency and money at inference for accuracy.

It matters because, for hard reasoning problems, spending more compute at inference can rival or exceed the gains from making the model itself larger. A smaller model that is allowed to think longer, by producing an extended chain-of-thought, sampling many independent solutions and voting, or searching over candidate steps, can match a much bigger model answering in one shot. This reframing, that inference is a place to invest compute and not just to harvest a trained model, is the basis of the reasoning-model wave and the rationale behind systems that visibly deliberate before answering.

The mechanisms come in several shapes. The simplest is sequential: let the model generate a long reasoning trace so it can work through intermediate steps before committing to a final answer. Another is parallel: sample many candidate solutions at a nonzero temperature and aggregate them, by majority vote or by scoring with a verifier model. A third is search: expand and prune a tree of reasoning steps, closer in spirit to beam search than to plain decoding. These can be layered, and a separate model is often trained to judge which candidate reasoning is actually correct.

The defining property is a smooth scaling curve. Accuracy tends to rise predictably as the compute budget per query grows, giving a knob that can be set per request according to how much the answer is worth, which makes inference efficiency directly valuable, since cheaper tokens mean more thinking for the same cost, and is why test-time scaling is usually deployed alongside serving optimizations like speculative-decoding and quantization. The cost is real, more latency and more spend on every hard query, so the budget is matched to the difficulty of the task rather than applied uniformly.

image.alt: A line chart with generation budget on a logarithmic x-axis and math test accuracy in percent on the y-axis. Several selection methods (majority vote, best-of-N weighted, and reward-model-based search) all rise steeply as the budget grows from a few to hundreds of samples, then flatten.
image.caption: Test-time compute spends more computation per query to get better answers. As the generation budget grows from a handful of samples to hundreds (x-axis, log scale), accuracy climbs and then flattens, a smooth scaling curve that turns inference into a dial: spend more thinking for harder questions. The gain comes from sampling and selecting, not from a larger model.
image.credit: Charlie Snell, Jaehoon Lee, Kelvin Xu, Aviral Kumar
